It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as **1xx** (Informational), **2xx** (Success, e.g., 200 OK), **3xx** (Redirection), **4xx** (Client Error, e.g., 40\",null,[\"95fc8323-1f8c-4f6a-bf3f-a85f3e020b4e\",\"c7dd0b8f-af4b-4f01-96eb-b65754eea67f\",3433107653],null,[[[[0,254,[[[0,4,[\"HTTP\",[true]]],[4,16,[\" stands for \"]],[16,43,[\"Hypertext Transfer Protocol\",[true]]],[43,101,[\" (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas). It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as **1xx** (Informational), **2xx** (Success, e.g., 200 OK), **3xx** (Redirection), **4xx** (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and **5xx** (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error\",null,[\"95fc8323-1f8c-4f6a-bf3f-a85f3e020b4e\",\"c7dd0b8f-af4b-4f01-96eb-b65754eea67f\",3433107653],null,[[[[0,254,[[[0,4,[\"HTTP\",[true]]],[4,16,[\" stands for \"]],[16,43,[\"Hypertext Transfer Protocol\",[true]]],[43,101,[\" (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas). It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as **1xx** (Informational), **2xx** (Success, e.g., 200 OK), **3xx** (Redirection), **4xx** (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and **5xx** (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) [7-9].\\n*   **Headers:** These are key-value pairs\",null,[\"95fc8323-1f8c-4f6a-bf3f-a85f3e020b4e\",\"c7dd0b8f-af4b-4f01-96eb-b65754eea67f\",3433107653],null,[[[[0,254,[[[0,4,[\"HTTP\",[true]]],[4,16,[\" stands for \"]],[16,43,[\"Hypertext Transfer Protocol\",[true]]],[43,101,[\" (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas). It is a foundational \"]],[101,130,[\"request and response protocol\",[true]]],[130,254,[\" used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server.\"]]]]],[254,546,[[[254,546,[\"While it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP).\"]]]]],[546,697,[[[546,580,[\"Technically, HTTP operates on the \"]],[580,608,[\"TCP transport layer protocol\",[true]]],[608,627,[\" (standardly using \"]],[627,634,[\"port 80\",[true]]],[634,697,[\" for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol.\"]]]]],[697,775,[[[697,775,[\"The core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\"]]]]],[775,1147,[[[775,783,[\"Methods:\",[true]]],[783,874,[\" These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include \"]],[874,877,[\"GET\",[true]]],[877,904,[\" (to retrieve a resource), \"]],[904,908,[\"POST\",[true]]],[908,939,[\" (to send data to the server), \"]],[939,942,[\"PUT\",[true]]],[942,979,[\" (to update or save a resource), and \"]],[979,985,[\"DELETE\",[true]]],[985,1147,[\". Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result).\"]]],[null,1],null,[null,null,0,{\"101\":\"•\",\"102\":1,\"103\":1,\"104\":0}]]],[1147,1442,[[[1147,1160,[\"Status Codes:\",[true]]],[1160,1276,[\" These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as \"]],[1276,1279,[\"1xx\",[true]]],[1279,1297,[\" (Informational), \"]],[1297,1300,[\"2xx\",[true]]],[1300,1326,[\" (Success, e.g., 200 OK), \"]],[1326,1329,[\"3xx\",[true]]],[1329,1345,[\" (Redirection), \"]],[1345,1348,[\"4xx\",[true]]],[1348,1390,[\" (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and \"]],[1390,1393,[\"5xx\",[true]]],[1393,1442,[\" (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error).\"]]],[null,1],null,[null,null,0,{\"101\":\"•\",\"102\":1,\"103\":2,\"104\":1}]]],[1442,1476,[[[1442,1450,[\"Headers:\",[true]]],[1450,1476,[\" These are key-value It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as **1xx** (Informational), **2xx** (Success, e.g., 200 OK), **3xx** (Redirection), **4xx** (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and **5xx** (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) [7-9].\\n*   **Headers:** These are key-value pairs that provide essential metadata about the transaction. For example, the `Host` header specifies the target domain, `Content-Type` dictates the format of\",null,[\"95fc8323-1f8c-4f6a-bf3f-a85f3e020b4e\",\"c7dd0b8f-af4b-4f01-96eb-b65754eea67f\",3433107653],null,[[[[0,254,[[[0,4,[\"HTTP\",[true]]],[4,16,[\" stands for \"]],[16,43,[\"Hypertext Transfer Protocol\",[true]]],[43,101,[\" (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas). It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as **1xx** (Informational), **2xx** (Success, e.g., 200 OK), **3xx** (Redirection), **4xx** (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and **5xx** (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) [7-9].\\n*   **Headers:** These are key-value pairs that provide essential metadata about the transaction. For example, the `Host` header specifies the target domain, `Content-Type` dictates the format of the data being sent (like `text/html` or `application/json`), and `Accept` tells the server what content formats\",null,[\"95fc8323-1f8c-4f6a-bf3f-a85f3e020b4e\",\"c7dd0b8f-af4b-4f01-96eb-b65754eea67f\",3433107653],null,[[[[0,254,[[[0,4,[\"HTTP\",[true]]],[4,16,[\" stands for \"]],[16,43,[\"Hypertext Transfer Protocol\",[true]]],[43,101,[\" (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas). It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as **1xx** (Informational), **2xx** (Success, e.g., 200 OK), **3xx** (Redirection), **4xx** (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and **5xx** (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) [7-9].\\n*   **Headers:** These are key-value pairs that provide essential metadata about the transaction. For example, the `Host` header specifies the target domain, `Content-Type` dictates the format of the data being sent (like `text/html` or `application/json`), and `Accept` tells the server what content formats the client can understand [10-13].\\n*   **Body:** The\",null,[\"95fc8323-1f8c-4f6a-bf3f-a85f3e020b4e\",\"c7dd0b8f-af4b-4f01-96eb-b65754eea67f\",3433107653],null,[[[[0,254,[[[0,4,[\"HTTP\",[true]]],[4,16,[\" stands for \"]],[16,43,[\"Hypertext Transfer Protocol\",[true]]],[43,101,[\" (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas). It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as **1xx** (Informational), **2xx** (Success, e.g., 200 OK), **3xx** (Redirection), **4xx** (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and **5xx** (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) [7-9].\\n*   **Headers:** These are key-value pairs that provide essential metadata about the transaction. For example, the `Host` header specifies the target domain, `Content-Type` dictates the format of the data being sent (like `text/html` or `application/json`), and `Accept` tells the server what content formats the client can understand [10-13].\\n*   **Body:** The actual payload or data being transferred. The way the body is interpreted, encoded, or split into chunks is defined by the headers [\",null,[\"95fc8323-1f8c-4f6a-bf3f-a85f3e020b4e\",\"c7dd0b8f-af4b-4f01-96eb-b65754eea67f\",3433107653],null,[[[[0,254,[[[0,4,[\"HTTP\",[true]]],[4,16,[\" stands for \"]],[16,43,[\"Hypertext Transfer Protocol\",[true]]],[43,101,[\" (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas). It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as **1xx** (Informational), **2xx** (Success, e.g., 200 OK), **3xx** (Redirection), **4xx** (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and **5xx** (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) [7-9].\\n*   **Headers:** These are key-value pairs that provide essential metadata about the transaction. For example, the `Host` header specifies the target domain, `Content-Type` dictates the format of the data being sent (like `text/html` or `application/json`), and `Accept` tells the server what content formats the client can understand [10-13].\\n*   **Body:** The actual payload or data being transferred. The way the body is interpreted, encoded, or split into chunks is defined by the headers [14-16].\\n\\n**Evolution of the Protocol:**\\nThe HTTP standard has evolved significantly over\",null,[\"95fc8323-1f8c-4f6a-bf3f-a85f3e020b4e\",\"c7dd0b8f-af4b-4f01-96eb-b65754eea67f\",3433107653],null,[[[[0,254,[[[0,4,[\"HTTP\",[true]]],[4,16,[\" stands for \"]],[16,43,[\"Hypertext Transfer Protocol\",[true]]],[43,101,[\" (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas). It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as **1xx** (Informational), **2xx** (Success, e.g., 200 OK), **3xx** (Redirection), **4xx** (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and **5xx** (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) [7-9].\\n*   **Headers:** These are key-value pairs that provide essential metadata about the transaction. For example, the `Host` header specifies the target domain, `Content-Type` dictates the format of the data being sent (like `text/html` or `application/json`), and `Accept` tells the server what content formats the client can understand [10-13].\\n*   **Body:** The actual payload or data being transferred. The way the body is interpreted, encoded, or split into chunks is defined by the headers [14-16].\\n\\n**Evolution of the Protocol:**\\nThe HTTP standard has evolved significantly over time, with major versions including 0.9, 1.0, 1.1, 2.0, and\",null,[\"95fc8323-1f8c-4f6a-bf3f-a85f3e020b4e\",\"c7dd0b8f-af4b-4f01-96eb-b65754eea67f\",3433107653],null,[[[[0,254,[[[0,4,[\"HTTP\",[true]]],[4,16,[\" stands for \"]],[16,43,[\"Hypertext Transfer Protocol\",[true]]],[43,101,[\" (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas). It is a foundational \"]],[101,130,[\"request and response protocol\",[true]]],[130,254,[\" used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server.\"]]]]],[254,546,[[[254,546,[\"While it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP).\"]]]]],[546,697,[[[546,580,[\"Technically, HTTP operates on the \"]],[580,608,[\"TCP transport layer protocol\",[true]]],[608,627,[\" (standardly using \"]],[627,634,[\"port 80\",[true]]],[634,697,[\" for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol.\"]]]]],[697,775,[[[697,775,[\"The core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\"]]]]],[775,1147,[[[775,783,[\"Methods:\",[true]]],[783,874,[\" These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include \"]],[874,877,[\"GET\",[true]]],[877,904,[\" (to retrieve a resource), \"]],[904,908,[\"POST\",[true]]],[908,939,[\" (to send data to the server), \"]],[939,942,[\"PUT\",[true]]],[942,979,[\" (to update or save a resource), and \"]],[979,985,[\"DELETE\",[true]]],[985,1147,[\". Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result).\"]]],[null,1],null,[null,null,0,{\"101\":\"•\",\"102\":1,\"103\":1,\"104\":0}]]],[1147,1442,[[[1147,1160,[\"Status Codes:\",[true]]],[1160,1276,[\" These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as \"]],[1276,1279,[\"1xx\",[true]]],[1279,1297,[\" (Informational), \"]],[1297,1300,[\"2xx\",[true]]],[1300,1326,[\" (Success, e.g., 200 OK), \"]],[1326,1329,[\"3xx\",[true]]],[1329,1345,[\" (Redirection), \"]],[1345,1348,[\"4xx\",[true]]],[1348,1390,[\" (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and \"]],[1390,1393,[\"5xx\",[true]]],[1393,1442,[\" (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error).\"]]],[null,1],null,[null,null,0,{\"101\":\"•\",\"102\":1,\"103\":2,\"104\":1}]]],[1442,1759,[[[1442,1450,[\"Headers:\",[true]]],[1450,1549,[\" These are key-value pairs that provide essential metadata about the transaction. For example, the \"]],[1549,1553,[\"Host\",[null,null,null,null,null,null,null,true]]],[1553,1590,[\" header specifies the target domain, \"]],[1590,1602,[\"Content-Type\",[null,null,null,null,null,null,null,true]]],[1602,1652,[\" dictates the format of the data being sent (like \"]],[1652,1661,[\"text/html\",[null,null,null,null,null,null,null,true]]],[1661,1665,[\" or \"]],[1665,1681,[\"application/json\",[null,null,null,null,null,null,null,true]]],[1681,1688,[\"), and \"]],[1688,1694,[\"Accept\",[null,null,null,null,null,null,null,true]]],[1694,1759,[\" tells the server what content formats the client can understand.\"]]],[null,1],null,[null,null,0,{\"101\":\"•\",\"102\":1,\"103\":3,\"104\":2}]]],[1759,1900,[[[1759,1764,[\"Body:\",[true]]],[1764,1900,[\" The actual payload or data being transferred. The way the body is interpreted, encoded, or split into chunks is defined by the It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as **1xx** (Informational), **2xx** (Success, e.g., 200 OK), **3xx** (Redirection), **4xx** (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and **5xx** (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) [7-9].\\n*   **Headers:** These are key-value pairs that provide essential metadata about the transaction. For example, the `Host` header specifies the target domain, `Content-Type` dictates the format of the data being sent (like `text/html` or `application/json`), and `Accept` tells the server what content formats the client can understand [10-13].\\n*   **Body:** The actual payload or data being transferred. The way the body is interpreted, encoded, or split into chunks is defined by the headers [14-16].\\n\\n**Evolution of the Protocol:**\\nThe HTTP standard has evolved significantly over time, with major versions including 0.9, 1.0, 1.1, 2.0, and 3.0 [4]. \\n*   **HTTP/1.1** introduced features like persistent connections\",null,[\"95fc8323-1f8c-4f6a-bf3f-a85f3e020b4e\",\"c7dd0b8f-af4b-4f01-96eb-b65754eea67f\",3433107653],null,[[[[0,254,[[[0,4,[\"HTTP\",[true]]],[4,16,[\" stands for \"]],[16,43,[\"Hypertext Transfer Protocol\",[true]]],[43,101,[\" (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas). It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as **1xx** (Informational), **2xx** (Success, e.g., 200 OK), **3xx** (Redirection), **4xx** (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and **5xx** (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) [7-9].\\n*   **Headers:** These are key-value pairs that provide essential metadata about the transaction. For example, the `Host` header specifies the target domain, `Content-Type` dictates the format of the data being sent (like `text/html` or `application/json`), and `Accept` tells the server what content formats the client can understand [10-13].\\n*   **Body:** The actual payload or data being transferred. The way the body is interpreted, encoded, or split into chunks is defined by the headers [14-16].\\n\\n**Evolution of the Protocol:**\\nThe HTTP standard has evolved significantly over time, with major versions including 0.9, 1.0, 1.1, 2.0, and 3.0 [4]. \\n*   **HTTP/1.1** introduced features like persistent connections to execute multiple requests without reopening the TCP connection every time [17, 18]. However, it struggled with issues\",null,[\"95fc8323-1f8c-4f6a-bf3f-a85f3e020b4e\",\"c7dd0b8f-af4b-4f01-96eb-b65754eea67f\",3433107653],null,[[[[0,254,[[[0,4,[\"HTTP\",[true]]],[4,16,[\" stands for \"]],[16,43,[\"Hypertext Transfer Protocol\",[true]]],[43,101,[\" (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas). For example, the \"]],[1549,1553,[\"Host\",[null,null,null,null,null,null,null,true]]],[1553,1590,[\" header specifies the target domain, \"]],[1590,1602,[\"Content-Type\",[null,null,null,null,null,null,null,true]]],[1602,1652,[\" dictates the format of the data being sent (like \"]],[1652,1661,[\"text/html\",[null,null,null,null,null,null,null,true]]],[1661,1665,[\" or \"]],[1665,1681,[\"application/json\",[null,null,null,null,null,null,null,true]]],[1681,1688,[\"), and \"]],[1688,1694,[\"Accept\",[null,null,null,null,null,null,null,true]]],[1694,1759,[\" tells the server what content formats the client can understand.\"]]],[null,1],null,[null,null,0,{\"101\":\"•\",\"102\":1,\"103\":3,\"104\":2}]]],[1759,1900,[[[1759,1764,[\"Body:\",[true]]],[1764,1900,[\" The actual payload or data being transferred. It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as **1xx** (Informational), **2xx** (Success, e.g., 200 OK), **3xx** (Redirection), **4xx** (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and **5xx** (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) [7-9].\\n*   **Headers:** These are key-value pairs that provide essential metadata about the transaction. For example, the `Host` header specifies the target domain, `Content-Type` dictates the format of the data being sent (like `text/html` or `application/json`), and `Accept` tells the server what content formats the client can understand [10-13].\\n*   **Body:** The actual payload or data being transferred. The way the body is interpreted, encoded, or split into chunks is defined by the headers [14-16].\\n\\n**Evolution of the Protocol:**\\nThe HTTP standard has evolved significantly over time, with major versions including 0.9, 1.0, 1.1, 2.0, and 3.0 [4]. \\n*   **HTTP/1.1** introduced features like persistent connections to execute multiple requests without reopening the TCP connection every time [17, 18]. However, it struggled with issues like \\\"Head-of-line blocking,\\\" where a single slow request could block subsequent ones [19]. \\n*   \",null,[\"95fc8323-1f8c-4f6a-bf3f-a85f3e020b4e\",\"c7dd0b8f-af4b-4f01-96eb-b65754eea67f\",3433107653],null,[[[[0,254,[[[0,4,[\"HTTP\",[true]]],[4,16,[\" stands for \"]],[16,43,[\"Hypertext Transfer Protocol\",[true]]],[43,101,[\" (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas). It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as **1xx** (Informational), **2xx** (Success, e.g., 200 OK), **3xx** (Redirection), **4xx** (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and **5xx** (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) [7-9].\\n*   **Headers:** These are key-value pairs that provide essential metadata about the transaction. For example, the `Host` header specifies the target domain, `Content-Type` dictates the format of the data being sent (like `text/html` or `application/json`), and `Accept` tells the server what content formats the client can understand [10-13].\\n*   **Body:** The actual payload or data being transferred. The way the body is interpreted, encoded, or split into chunks is defined by the headers [14-16].\\n\\n**Evolution of the Protocol:**\\nThe HTTP standard has evolved significantly over time, with major versions including 0.9, 1.0, 1.1, 2.0, and 3.0 [4]. \\n*   **HTTP/1.1** introduced features like persistent connections to execute multiple requests without reopening the TCP connection every time [17, 18]. However, it struggled with issues like \\\"Head-of-line blocking,\\\" where a single slow request could block subsequent ones [19]. \\n*   **HTTP/2** was introduced as a binary protocol to solve these performance issues. It introduced **multiplexing**,\",null,[\"95fc8323-1f8c-4f6a-bf3f-a85f3e020b4e\",\"c7dd0b8f-af4b-4f01-96eb-b65754eea67f\",3433107653],null,[[[[0,254,[[[0,4,[\"HTTP\",[true]]],[4,16,[\" stands for \"]],[16,43,[\"Hypertext Transfer Protocol\",[true]]],[43,101,[\" (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas). It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as **1xx** (Informational), **2xx** (Success, e.g., 200 OK), **3xx** (Redirection), **4xx** (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and **5xx** (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) [7-9].\\n*   **Headers:** These are key-value pairs that provide essential metadata about the transaction. For example, the `Host` header specifies the target domain, `Content-Type` dictates the format of the data being sent (like `text/html` or `application/json`), and `Accept` tells the server what content formats the client can understand [10-13].\\n*   **Body:** The actual payload or data being transferred. The way the body is interpreted, encoded, or split into chunks is defined by the headers [14-16].\\n\\n**Evolution of the Protocol:**\\nThe HTTP standard has evolved significantly over time, with major versions including 0.9, 1.0, 1.1, 2.0, and 3.0 [4]. \\n*   **HTTP/1.1** introduced features like persistent connections to execute multiple requests without reopening the TCP connection every time [17, 18]. However, it struggled with issues like \\\"Head-of-line blocking,\\\" where a single slow request could block subsequent ones [19]. \\n*   **HTTP/2** was introduced as a binary protocol to solve these performance issues. It introduced **multiplexing**, allowing multiple data streams to be transferred simultaneously over a single connection, along with features like header compression and server push [20]\",null,[\"95fc8323-1f8c-4f6a-bf3f-a85f3e020b4e\",\"c7dd0b8f-af4b-4f01-96eb-b65754eea67f\",3433107653],null,[[[[0,254,[[[0,4,[\"HTTP\",[true]]],[4,16,[\" stands for \"]],[16,43,[\"Hypertext Transfer Protocol\",[true]]],[43,101,[\" (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas). It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as **1xx** (Informational), **2xx** (Success, e.g., 200 OK), **3xx** (Redirection), **4xx** (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and **5xx** (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) [7-9].\\n*   **Headers:** These are key-value pairs that provide essential metadata about the transaction. For example, the `Host` header specifies the target domain, `Content-Type` dictates the format of the data being sent (like `text/html` or `application/json`), and `Accept` tells the server what content formats the client can understand [10-13].\\n*   **Body:** The actual payload or data being transferred. The way the body is interpreted, encoded, or split into chunks is defined by the headers [14-16].\\n\\n**Evolution of the Protocol:**\\nThe HTTP standard has evolved significantly over time, with major versions including 0.9, 1.0, 1.1, 2.0, and 3.0 [4]. \\n*   **HTTP/1.1** introduced features like persistent connections to execute multiple requests without reopening the TCP connection every time [17, 18]. However, it struggled with issues like \\\"Head-of-line blocking,\\\" where a single slow request could block subsequent ones [19]. \\n*   **HTTP/2** was introduced as a binary protocol to solve these performance issues. It introduced **multiplexing**, allowing multiple data streams to be transferred simultaneously over a single connection, along with features like header compression and server push [20, 21].\",null,[\"95fc8323-1f8c-4f6a-bf3f-a85f3e020b4e\",\"c7dd0b8f-af4b-4f01-96eb-b65754eea67f\",3433107653],null,[[[[0,254,[[[0,4,[\"HTTP\",[true]]],[4,16,[\" stands for \"]],[16,43,[\"Hypertext Transfer Protocol\",[true]]],[43,101,[\" (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas).    ]]73406[    [        "wrb.fr",        null,        "[[\"**HTTP** stands for **Hypertext Transfer Protocol** (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas) [1]. It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as **1xx** (Informational), **2xx** (Success, e.g., 200 OK), **3xx** (Redirection), **4xx** (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and **5xx** (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) [7-9].\\n*   **Headers:** These are key-value pairs that provide essential metadata about the transaction. For example, the `Host` header specifies the target domain, `Content-Type` dictates the format of the data being sent (like `text/html` or `application/json`), and `Accept` tells the server what content formats the client can understand [10-13].\\n*   **Body:** The actual payload or data being transferred. The way the body is interpreted, encoded, or split into chunks is defined by the headers [14-16].\\n\\n**Evolution of the Protocol:**\\nThe HTTP standard has evolved significantly over time, with major versions including 0.9, 1.0, 1.1, 2.0, and 3.0 [4]. \\n*   **HTTP/1.1** introduced features like persistent connections to execute multiple requests without reopening the TCP connection every time [17, 18]. However, it struggled with issues like \\\"Head-of-line blocking,\\\" where a single slow request could block subsequent ones [19]. \\n*   **HTTP/2** was introduced as a binary protocol to solve these performance issues. It introduced **multiplexing**, allowing multiple data streams to be transferred simultaneously over a single connection, along with features like header compression and server push [20, 21].\",null,[\"95fc8323-1f8c-4f6a-bf3f-a85f3e020b4e\",\"c7dd0b8f-af4b-4f01-96eb-b65754eea67f\",3433107653],null,[[[[0,254,[[[0,4,[\"HTTP\",[true]]],[4,16,[\" stands for \"]],[16,43,[\"Hypertext Transfer Protocol\",[true]]],[43,101,[\" (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas).    ]]73406[    [        "wrb.fr",        null,        "[[\"**HTTP** stands for **Hypertext Transfer Protocol** (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas) [1]. It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as **1xx** (Informational), **2xx** (Success, e.g., 200 OK), **3xx** (Redirection), **4xx** (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and **5xx** (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) [7-9].\\n*   **Headers:** These are key-value pairs that provide essential metadata about the transaction. For example, the `Host` header specifies the target domain, `Content-Type` dictates the format of the data being sent (like `text/html` or `application/json`), and `Accept` tells the server what content formats the client can understand [10-13].\\n*   **Body:** The actual payload or data being transferred. The way the body is interpreted, encoded, or split into chunks is defined by the headers [14-16].\\n\\n**Evolution of the Protocol:**\\nThe HTTP standard has evolved significantly over time, with major versions including 0.9, 1.0, 1.1, 2.0, and 3.0 [4]. \\n*   **HTTP/1.1** introduced features like persistent connections to execute multiple requests without reopening the TCP connection every time [17, 18]. However, it struggled with issues like \\\"Head-of-line blocking,\\\" where a single slow request could block subsequent ones [19]. \\n*   **HTTP/2** was introduced as a binary protocol to solve these performance issues. It introduced **multiplexing**, allowing multiple data streams to be transferred simultaneously over a single connection, along with features like header compression and server push [20, 21].\",null,[\"95fc8323-1f8c-4f6a-bf3f-a85f3e020b4e\",\"c7dd0b8f-af4b-4f01-96eb-b65754eea67f\",3433107653],null,[[[[0,254,[[[0,4,[\"HTTP\",[true]]],[4,16,[\" stands for \"]],[16,43,[\"Hypertext Transfer Protocol\",[true]]],[43,101,[\" (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas).    ]]73708[    [        "wrb.fr",        null,        "[[\"**HTTP** stands for **Hypertext Transfer Protocol** (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas) [1]. It is a foundational **request and response protocol** used for communication across the World Wide Web, typically between a User Agent (like a web browser) and an Origin Server [2]. \\n\\nWhile it was historically designed to transfer simple hypertext, modern HTTP is capable of transmitting almost any type of digital information in both directions, including images (PNG, JPG), videos (MP4), application scripts (JavaScript), styling sheets (CSS), and compressed archives (ZIP) [1]. \\n\\nTechnically, HTTP operates on the **TCP transport layer protocol** (standardly using **port 80** for web browsing) and utilizes the IP internet layer protocol [3, 4]. \\n\\nThe core structure of HTTP communication is built upon several key components:\\n*   **Methods:** These define the action the client wants to perform on a resource. Common methods include **GET** (to retrieve a resource), **POST** (to send data to the server), **PUT** (to update or save a resource), and **DELETE** [5]. Certain methods are considered \\\"safe\\\" (they do not change the server's state, like GET) and \\\"idempotent\\\" (executing them multiple times yields the same result) [6].\\n*   **Status Codes:** These indicate the result of the server's attempt to process the request. They are grouped into categories such as **1xx** (Informational), **2xx** (Success, e.g., 200 OK), **3xx** (Redirection), **4xx** (Client Error, e.g., 404 Not Found), and **5xx** (Server Error, e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) [7-9].\\n*   **Headers:** These are key-value pairs that provide essential metadata about the transaction. For example, the `Host` header specifies the target domain, `Content-Type` dictates the format of the data being sent (like `text/html` or `application/json`), and `Accept` tells the server what content formats the client can understand [10-13].\\n*   **Body:** The actual payload or data being transferred. The way the body is interpreted, encoded, or split into chunks is defined by the headers [14-16].\\n\\n**Evolution of the Protocol:**\\nThe HTTP standard has evolved significantly over time, with major versions including 0.9, 1.0, 1.1, 2.0, and 3.0 [4]. \\n*   **HTTP/1.1** introduced features like persistent connections to execute multiple requests without reopening the TCP connection every time [17, 18]. However, it struggled with issues like \\\"Head-of-line blocking,\\\" where a single slow request could block subsequent ones [19]. \\n*   **HTTP/2** was introduced as a binary protocol to solve these performance issues. It introduced **multiplexing**, allowing multiple data streams to be transferred simultaneously over a single connection, along with features like header compression and server push [20, 21].\",null,[\"95fc8323-1f8c-4f6a-bf3f-a85f3e020b4e\",\"c7dd0b8f-af4b-4f01-96eb-b65754eea67f\",3433107653],null,[[[[0,254,[[[0,4,[\"HTTP\",[true]]],[4,16,[\" stands for \"]],[16,43,[\"Hypertext Transfer Protocol\",[true]]],[43,101,[\" (Hiperteksto perdavimo protokolas).
